case_name: Cobalt removal from surface water for drinking water production with minimal chemical use
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for cobalt-contaminated surface water intended for drinking water supply under a minimal-chemical-use preference.

input_data.scenario_background: A small drinking water utility draws from a river reach influenced by upstream mining-impacted runoff and has observed elevated dissolved cobalt in the raw water. The utility already uses intake screening and basic filtration, but wants a proven treatment upgrade that minimizes routine chemical addition. Natural organic matter and moderate seasonal turbidity may affect cobalt removal performance, so the preferred solution should be robust to typical surface-water variability.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable cobalt reduction suitable for drinking water production and maintain stable finished water quality under variable surface-water conditions.
secondary_goals: favor proven and operationally simple technologies

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: backwash waste handling is available but continuous high-volume chemical sludge generation is undesirable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: system should tolerate seasonal turbidity and natural organic matter variation with stable performance

core_unit_functions.pre_filtration: reduce suspended solids load and protect downstream media from rapid fouling
core_unit_functions.manganese_oxide_or_iron_oxide_adsorptive: remove dissolved cobalt by surface complexation and adsorption onto metal oxide surfaces
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain media fines and polish remaining particulate matter
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety for drinking water supply

applicability_rationale: cobalt is the primary contaminant and is largely dissolved, making metal-oxide-based adsorption a strong conventional option
the utility prefers minimal chemical use, and fixed-bed adsorptive media can operate with low routine chemical dosing compared with full chemical precipitation
moderate turbidity and DOC indicate a need for pretreatment to protect media and maintain run length
this approach is simpler and lower energy than membrane systems while still being well aligned with drinking-water treatment practice
potential_risks: natural organic matter and competing metals can reduce adsorption capacity and shorten media life
influent turbidity spikes may foul the media if pretreatment is inadequate
media exhaustion requires monitoring and timely replacement or regeneration planning

core_unit_functions.aeration_or_oxidation: promote favorable surface reactions and improve removal of associated reduced metals with limited reagent demand
core_unit_functions.roughing_or_dual_media_filtration: remove particulate matter and reduce fouling potential ahead of specialty media
core_unit_functions.manganese_dioxide_coated_media: adsorb and retain dissolved cobalt on catalytic oxide-coated surfaces

applicability_rationale: oxide-coated media are widely used for metal control and can fit a low-chemical operating philosophy
surface water matrix effects are addressed by adding front-end solids removal before the specialty media stage
the train remains relatively simple for a small utility with conventional filtration experience
potential_risks: cobalt removal may be less predictable than for iron or manganese and may require pilot confirmation
if oxidation conditions are poorly controlled, removal benefit may be limited
coated media performance can decline under high DOC loading

core_unit_functions.pH_adjustment: raise pH into a range that favors cobalt hydroxide formation
core_unit_functions.hydroxide_precipitation: convert dissolved cobalt into removable insoluble solids
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove remaining suspended solids and polish effluent

applicability_rationale: chemical precipitation is a well-established route for dissolved metal removal
it can achieve substantial cobalt reduction when pH control and solids separation are well managed
this route is a conventional fallback if adsorption capacity is insufficient or cobalt concentrations increase
potential_risks: requires more routine chemical addition than preferred in this case
produces more sludge and increases residuals handling burden
DOC and fine colloids can interfere with settling and filtration performance

core_unit_functions.coagulation: destabilize natural organic matter and some colloid-associated metals
core_unit_functions.flocculation: form settleable flocs for downstream solids removal
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove flocculated solids and associated particulate cobalt fraction
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_or_biologicall: reduce organics and provide some polishing of residual particulate or complexed contaminants
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure pathogen control in finished water

applicability_rationale: surface-water NOM and turbidity can be addressed effectively with conventional clarification
this train can help with matrix effects and improve overall finished water quality
however, it is not the strongest direct method for dissolved cobalt compared with dedicated adsorptive media or precipitation
potential_risks: dissolved cobalt removal may be incomplete because cobalt is mostly not particle-bound in this case
chemical demand conflicts with the minimal-chemical-use preference
performance depends on cobalt speciation and association with organics or particulates

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: reject dissolved cobalt and many other dissolved constituents through membrane separation
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: high-pressure membranes are technically capable of strong dissolved metal removal
they may be considered if broader dissolved contaminant control is also required
for this case, they are not the preferred first choice because cobalt is the main target and chemical-minimizing, lower-complexity options are available
potential_risks: concentrate management is a major burden for inland utilities
membrane fouling risk is elevated by surface-water turbidity and NOM unless substantial pretreatment is added
high energy and maintenance needs are disproportionate for an otherwise straightforward metal-removal case

preferred_rank_pattern: adsorptive_metal_oxide_media_with_basic_pretreatment should rank first under minimal_chemical_use conditions
chemical_precipitation may appear but should rank below lower-chemical adsorptive options
high_pressure_membranes should not rank first for this single-contaminant drinking water case

must_reflect: minimal routine chemical dosing is preferred
surface-water turbidity and DOC require attention as matrix effects
moderate operational capability favors robust and established treatment trains

must_contain: why adsorptive oxide media are suitable for dissolved cobalt control
why pretreatment is needed to protect media in surface water with turbidity and NOM
why precipitation ranks lower despite being proven for metals
why membrane options rank lowest under the stated constraints

should_mention: competition from natural organic matter or other metals for adsorption sites
concentrate disposal challenges for membrane processes
